---
title: "week6_Visualizing with ggplot2"
author: "Macarena López-Mayorga"
date: 2026-05-05
format: pdf
---
First, we have to load dplyr

```{r}
#| eval: FALSE
library(dplyr)
```

Attaching package: ‘dplyr’

The following objects are masked from ‘package:stats’:

    filter, lag

The following objects are masked from ‘package:base’:

    intersect, setdiff, setequal, union

This message means that R is masking (or hiding) the base R stats and base package functions with identical names to prioritize the functions of dplyr when code is run.

Now, we are going to work with dataset from week04

```{r}
#| eval: false
StorageLocation <- file.path("C:/Users/Macarena/CytometryInR/course/04_IntroToTidyverse/data")
StorageLocation
```

```{r}
#| eval: false
TheCSV <- list.files(StorageLocation, full.names=TRUE)
TheCSV
```

```{r}
#| eval: false
Data <- read.csv(TheCSV, check.names=FALSE)
head(Data, 3)
```
      bid timepoint Condition
1 INF0052         0      Ctrl
2 INF0100         0      Ctrl
3 INF0100         4      Ctrl
        Date infant_sex  ptype    root
1 2025-07-26       Male HEU-hi 2098368
2 2025-07-26       Male HEU-lo 2020184
3 2025-07-26       Male HEU-lo 1155040
  singletsFSC singletsSSC singletsSSCB
1     1894070     1666179      1537396
2     1791890     1697083      1579098
3     1033320      875465       845446
       CD45 NotMonocytes nonDebris
1 0.5952943    0.8820349 0.8627649
2 0.9106762    0.9052256 0.8602660
3 0.9705765    0.9845400 0.9578793
  lymphocytes      live      Dump+
1   0.6420138 0.9020581 0.21090996
2   0.2145848 0.8908981 0.06252775
3   0.7403110 0.8757665 0.20023803
      Dump-    Tcells        Vd2+
1 0.6911482 0.2804264 0.008120361
2 0.8283703 0.6748298 0.007265620
3 0.6755285 0.6119129 0.004651313
       Vd2-     Va7.2+    Va7.2-
1 0.9918796 0.01448070 0.9773989
2 0.9927344 0.01577499 0.9769594
3 0.9953487 0.01579402 0.9795547
       CD4+      CD4-      CD8+
1 0.6341164 0.3432825 0.2734826
2 0.6119112 0.3650482 0.3357696
3 0.6639621 0.3155925 0.2862104
        CD8- Tcells_count
1 0.06979990       164771
2 0.02927858       208241
3 0.02938209       371723
  lymphocytes_count  Monocytes
1            587573 0.11796509
2            308583 0.09477437
3            607477 0.01545999
      Debris CD45_count
1 0.13723513     915203
2 0.13973396    1438047
3 0.04212072     820570

And now we are going to determine what type of values contains each column:

```{r}
#| eval: false
str(Data)
``` 
'data.frame':	196 obs. of  31 variables:
 $ bid              : chr  "INF0052" "INF0100" "INF0100" "INF0100" ...
 $ timepoint        : int  0 0 4 9 0 4 9 4 9 0 ...
 $ Condition        : chr  "Ctrl" "Ctrl" "Ctrl" "Ctrl" ...
 $ Date             : chr  "2025-07-26" "2025-07-26" "2025-07-26" "2025-07-26" ...
 $ infant_sex       : chr  "Male" "Male" "Male" "Male" ...
 $ ptype            : chr  "HEU-hi" "HEU-lo" "HEU-lo" "HEU-lo" ...
 $ root             : int  2098368 2020184 1155040 358624 1362216 1044808 1434840 972056 1521928 2363512 ...
 $ singletsFSC      : int  1894070 1791890 1033320 328624 1206309 917398 1265022 875707 1359574 2136616 ...
 $ singletsSSC      : int  1666179 1697083 875465 289327 1032946 735579 988445 767323 1175755 1875394 ...
 $ singletsSSCB     : int  1537396 1579098 845446 276289 982736 685592 940454 718000 1097478 1732620 ...
 $ CD45             : num  0.595 0.911 0.971 0.982 0.957 ...
 $ NotMonocytes     : num  0.882 0.905 0.985 0.986 0.956 ...
 $ nonDebris        : num  0.863 0.86 0.958 0.941 0.841 ...
 $ lymphocytes      : num  0.642 0.215 0.74 0.651 0.705 ...
 $ live             : num  0.902 0.891 0.876 0.915 0.895 ...
 $ Dump+            : num  0.2109 0.0625 0.2002 0.2147 0.3383 ...
 $ Dump-            : num  0.691 0.828 0.676 0.701 0.557 ...
 $ Tcells           : num  0.28 0.675 0.612 0.631 0.44 ...
 $ Vd2+             : num  0.00812 0.00727 0.00465 0.01135 0.00475 ...
 $ Vd2-             : num  0.992 0.993 0.995 0.989 0.995 ...
 $ Va7.2+           : num  0.0145 0.0158 0.0158 0.017 0.0133 ...
 $ Va7.2-           : num  0.977 0.977 0.98 0.972 0.982 ...
 $ CD4+             : num  0.634 0.612 0.664 0.438 0.739 ...
 $ CD4-             : num  0.343 0.365 0.316 0.534 0.243 ...
 $ CD8+             : num  0.273 0.336 0.286 0.486 0.195 ...
 $ CD8-             : num  0.0698 0.0293 0.0294 0.0476 0.0476 ...
 $ Tcells_count     : int  164771 208241 371723 111552 291777 271870 487937 220634 415867 184930 ...
 $ lymphocytes_count: int  587573 308583 607477 176662 663667 510730 726238 451047 710964 652155 ...
 $ Monocytes        : num  0.118 0.0948 0.0155 0.0145 0.0444 ...
 $ Debris           : num  0.1372 0.1397 0.0421 0.0587 0.1592 ...
 $ CD45_count       : int  915203 1438047 820570 271304 940733 675857 921660 701657 1066884 1017713 ...
 The type of values that a column contains are particularly important to be aware of when working with ggplot2 plots. For example, we can see that several columns contain character values:
 
 ```{r}
 #| eval: false
 Data |> select(bid, Condition, Date, infant_sex, ptype) |> head(6) |> str()
 ```
 'data.frame':	6 obs. of  5 variables:
 $ bid       : chr  "INF0052" "INF0100" "INF0100" "INF0100" ...
 $ Condition : chr  "Ctrl" "Ctrl" "Ctrl" "Ctrl" ...
 $ Date      : chr  "2025-07-26" "2025-07-26" "2025-07-26" "2025-07-26" ...
 $ infant_sex: chr  "Male" "Male" "Male" "Male" ...
 $ ptype     : chr  "HEU-hi" "HEU-lo" "HEU-lo" "HEU-lo" ...

 This is other way to do same but using tidyselect package (dplyr):
 
 ```{r}
 #| eval: false
 Data |> select(where(is.character)) |> head(6) |> str()
 ```
 'data.frame':	6 obs. of  5 variables:
 $ bid       : chr  "INF0052" "INF0100" "INF0100" "INF0100" ...
 $ Condition : chr  "Ctrl" "Ctrl" "Ctrl" "Ctrl" ...
 $ Date      : chr  "2025-07-26" "2025-07-26" "2025-07-26" "2025-07-26" ...
 $ infant_sex: chr  "Male" "Male" "Male" "Male" ...
 $ ptype     : chr  "HEU-hi" "HEU-lo" "HEU-lo" "HEU-lo" ...
> Data |> select(where(is.character)) |> head(6) |> str()
'data.frame':	6 obs. of  5 variables:
 $ bid       : chr  "INF0052" "INF0100" "INF0100" "INF0100" ...
 $ Condition : chr  "Ctrl" "Ctrl" "Ctrl" "Ctrl" ...
 $ Date      : chr  "2025-07-26" "2025-07-26" "2025-07-26" "2025-07-26" ...
 $ infant_sex: chr  "Male" "Male" "Male" "Male" ...
 $ ptype     : chr  "HEU-hi" "HEU-lo" "HEU-lo" "HEU-lo" ...
 
 We can also ask for numeric values or logical values:
 ```{r}
 #| eval: false
 Data |> select(where(is.numeric)) |> head(6) |> str()
 ```
 'data.frame':	6 obs. of  26 variables:
 $ timepoint        : int  0 0 4 9 0 4
 $ root             : int  2098368 2020184 1155040 358624 1362216 1044808
 $ singletsFSC      : int  1894070 1791890 1033320 328624 1206309 917398
 $ singletsSSC      : int  1666179 1697083 875465 289327 1032946 735579
 $ singletsSSCB     : int  1537396 1579098 845446 276289 982736 685592
 $ CD45             : num  0.595 0.911 0.971 0.982 0.957 ...
 $ NotMonocytes     : num  0.882 0.905 0.985 0.986 0.956 ...
 $ nonDebris        : num  0.863 0.86 0.958 0.941 0.841 ...
 $ lymphocytes      : num  0.642 0.215 0.74 0.651 0.705 ...
 $ live             : num  0.902 0.891 0.876 0.915 0.895 ...
 $ Dump+            : num  0.2109 0.0625 0.2002 0.2147 0.3383 ...
 $ Dump-            : num  0.691 0.828 0.676 0.701 0.557 ...
 $ Tcells           : num  0.28 0.675 0.612 0.631 0.44 ...
 $ Vd2+             : num  0.00812 0.00727 0.00465 0.01135 0.00475 ...
 $ Vd2-             : num  0.992 0.993 0.995 0.989 0.995 ...
 $ Va7.2+           : num  0.0145 0.0158 0.0158 0.017 0.0133 ...
 $ Va7.2-           : num  0.977 0.977 0.98 0.972 0.982 ...
 $ CD4+             : num  0.634 0.612 0.664 0.438 0.739 ...
 $ CD4-             : num  0.343 0.365 0.316 0.534 0.243 ...
 $ CD8+             : num  0.273 0.336 0.286 0.486 0.195 ...
 $ CD8-             : num  0.0698 0.0293 0.0294 0.0476 0.0476 ...
 $ Tcells_count     : int  164771 208241 371723 111552 291777 271870
 $ lymphocytes_count: int  587573 308583 607477 176662 663667 510730
 $ Monocytes        : num  0.118 0.0948 0.0155 0.0145 0.0444 ...
 $ Debris           : num  0.1372 0.1397 0.0421 0.0587 0.1592 ...
 $ CD45_count       : int  915203 1438047 820570 271304 940733 675857
 
 ```{r}
 #| eval: false
 Data |> select(where(is.logical)) |> head(6) |> str()
 ```
 'data.frame':	6 obs. of  0 variables

 # Reformatting Column Value Types
 Date column is currently consider as a character value:
 
 ```{r}
 #| eval: false
 Data |> select(Date) |> str()
 ```
 'data.frame':	196 obs. of  1 variable:
 $ Date: chr  "2025-07-26" "2025-07-26" "2025-07-26" "2025-07-26" ...
 
 Character values when plotting are generally treated as categorical factors. When plotted, these will arrange according to alphabetical order. In this particular case, this may not be an issue:
 
 ```{r}
 #| eval: false
 Data |> pull(Date) |> sort() |> unique() 
 ```
 [1] "2025-07-26" "2025-07-29" "2025-07-31" "2025-08-05"
[5] "2025-08-07" "2025-08-22" "2025-08-28" "2025-08-30"

However, if year/month/day is formatted differently, as character values, the alphabetical reordering could result in your dates being scrambled:

```{r}
#| eval: false
AlternateFormat <- c("26-07-2025", "27-08-2019", "09-09-2025", "16-03-2026")
AlternateFormat |> sort() |> unique()
```
[1] "09-09-2025" "16-03-2026" "26-07-2025" "27-08-2019"

Because of this, I generally recommend reformatting these character type values over to Date type values. I will generally use the tidyverse lubridate package, as it has various functions that can handle the date format variation. In this case, since the character values appear year-month-day, we can use the ymd() function to update the column type:

```{r}
#| eval: false
library(lubridate)
```
Attaching package: ‘lubridate’

The following objects are masked from ‘package:base’:

    date, intersect, setdiff, union

```{r}
#| eval: false
Data$Date <- ymd(Data$Date)
str(Data[,1:5])
```
'data.frame':	196 obs. of  5 variables:
 $ bid       : chr  "INF0052" "INF0100" "INF0100" "INF0100" ...
 $ timepoint : int  0 0 4 9 0 4 9 4 9 0 ...
 $ Condition : chr  "Ctrl" "Ctrl" "Ctrl" "Ctrl" ...
 $ Date      : Date, format: "2025-07-26" ...
 $ infant_sex: chr  "Male" "Male" "Male" "Male" ...

 You can notice, str() now returns the column type as “Date”.

 # Factors

 For columns with numeric type values, we previously discussed the difference between integer (ie. whole) and double (containing decimal point) values. When it comes to plotting using ggplot, numeric values will generally be treated as continuous values unless we specify they should be treated as categorical values for plotting.

For example, if we look at the Timepoint column, we can see its numeric values are discrete, corresponding to the timepoint in months when the blood sample was collected (0, 4, 9):

```{r}
#| eval: false
Data |> pull(timepoint) |> unique()
```
[1] 0 4 9

If we want to avoid having ggplot treating these values as continuous, and instead see them as categorical, we will need to specify to R to treat these as such. We can do this by converting them into factors, using the factor() function:

```{r}
#| eval: false
Data1 <- Data
Data1$timepoint <- factor(Data1$timepoint)
str(Data1[,1:3])
```
'data.frame':	196 obs. of  3 variables:
 $ bid      : chr  "INF0052" "INF0100" "INF0100" "INF0100" ...
 $ timepoint: Factor w/ 3 levels "0","4","9": 1 1 2 3 1 2 3 2 3 1 ...
 $ Condition: chr  "Ctrl" "Ctrl" "Ctrl" "Ctrl" ...

  Let’s try creating a boxplot, with the different timepoints on the x-axis, and the frequency of T cells in the CD45+ gate on the y-axis. Let’s go ahead and actually convert timepoint over to a categorical factor for this example, and mutate the frequency column:
  
  ```{r}
  #| eval: false
  Data <- Data |> 
    mutate(TcellProportion=Tcells_count/CD45_count) |>
    mutate(TcellFrequency=TcellProportion *100) |>
    mutate(TcellFrequency=round(TcellFrequency, 1))

Data$timepoint <- factor(Data$timepoint)
  ```
  
  ```{r}
  #| eval: false
  library(ggplot2)
  ```
  
  ```{r}
  #| eval: false
  Plot <- ggplot(Data)
  Plot
  ```
  (the result is a grey screen)

  As you can see, nothing is yet outputted at this stage. Let’s however peak behind the curtain.
  
  ```{r}
  #| eval: false
  str(Plot)
  ```
  <ggplot2::ggplot>
 @ data       :'data.frame':	196 obs. of  33 variables:
 .. $ bid              : chr  "INF0052" "INF0100" "INF0100" "INF0100" ...
 .. $ timepoint        : Factor w/ 3 levels "0","4","9": 1 1 2 3 1 2 3 2 3 1 ...
 .. $ Condition        : chr  "Ctrl" "Ctrl" "Ctrl" "Ctrl" ...
 .. $ Date             : Date, format: "2025-07-26" ...
 .. $ infant_sex       : chr  "Male" "Male" "Male" "Male" ...
 .. $ ptype            : chr  "HEU-hi" "HEU-lo" "HEU-lo" "HEU-lo" ...
 .. $ root             : int  2098368 2020184 1155040 358624 1362216 1044808 1434840 972056 1521928 2363512 ...
 .. $ singletsFSC      : int  1894070 1791890 1033320 328624 1206309 917398 1265022 875707 1359574 2136616 ...
 .. $ singletsSSC      : int  1666179 1697083 875465 289327 1032946 735579 988445 767323 1175755 1875394 ...
 .. $ singletsSSCB     : int  1537396 1579098 845446 276289 982736 685592 940454 718000 1097478 1732620 ...
 .. $ CD45             : num  0.595 0.911 0.971 0.982 0.957 ...
 .. $ NotMonocytes     : num  0.882 0.905 0.985 0.986 0.956 ...
 .. $ nonDebris        : num  0.863 0.86 0.958 0.941 0.841 ...
 .. $ lymphocytes      : num  0.642 0.215 0.74 0.651 0.705 ...
 .. $ live             : num  0.902 0.891 0.876 0.915 0.895 ...
 .. $ Dump+            : num  0.2109 0.0625 0.2002 0.2147 0.3383 ...
 .. $ Dump-            : num  0.691 0.828 0.676 0.701 0.557 ...
 .. $ Tcells           : num  0.28 0.675 0.612 0.631 0.44 ...
 .. $ Vd2+             : num  0.00812 0.00727 0.00465 0.01135 0.00475 ...
 .. $ Vd2-             : num  0.992 0.993 0.995 0.989 0.995 ...
 .. $ Va7.2+           : num  0.0145 0.0158 0.0158 0.017 0.0133 ...
 .. $ Va7.2-           : num  0.977 0.977 0.98 0.972 0.982 ...
 .. $ CD4+             : num  0.634 0.612 0.664 0.438 0.739 ...
 .. $ CD4-             : num  0.343 0.365 0.316 0.534 0.243 ...
 .. $ CD8+             : num  0.273 0.336 0.286 0.486 0.195 ...
 .. $ CD8-             : num  0.0698 0.0293 0.0294 0.0476 0.0476 ...
 .. $ Tcells_count     : int  164771 208241 371723 111552 291777 271870 487937 220634 415867 184930 ...
 .. $ lymphocytes_count: int  587573 308583 607477 176662 663667 510730 726238 451047 710964 652155 ...
 .. $ Monocytes        : num  0.118 0.0948 0.0155 0.0145 0.0444 ...
 .. $ Debris           : num  0.1372 0.1397 0.0421 0.0587 0.1592 ...
 .. $ CD45_count       : int  915203 1438047 820570 271304 940733 675857 921660 701657 1066884 1017713 ...
 .. $ TcellProportion  : num  0.18 0.145 0.453 0.411 0.31 ...
 .. $ TcellFrequency   : num  18 14.5 45.3 41.1 31 40.2 52.9 31.4 39 18.2 ...
 @ layers     : list()
 @ scales     :Classes 'ScalesList', 'ggproto', 'gg' <ggproto object: Class ScalesList, gg>
    add: function
    add_defaults: function
    add_missing: function
    backtransform_df: function
    clone: function
    find: function
    get_scales: function
    has_scale: function
    input: function
    map_df: function
    n: function
    non_position_scales: function
    scales: NULL
    set_palettes: function
    train_df: function
    transform_df: function
    super:  <ggproto object: Class ScalesList, gg> 
 @ guides     :Classes 'Guides', 'ggproto', 'gg' <ggproto object: Class Guides, gg>
    add: function
    assemble: function
    build: function
    draw: function
    get_custom: function
    get_guide: function
    get_params: function
    get_position: function
    guides: NULL
    merge: function
    missing: <ggproto object: Class GuideNone, Guide, gg>
        add_title: function
        arrange_layout: function
        assemble_drawing: function
        available_aes: any
        build_decor: function
        build_labels: function
        build_ticks: function
        build_title: function
        draw: function
        draw_early_exit: function
        elements: list
        extract_decor: function
        extract_key: function
        extract_params: function
        get_layer_key: function
        hashables: list
        measure_grobs: function
        merge: function
        override_elements: function
        params: list
        process_layers: function
        setup_elements: function
        setup_params: function
        train: function
        transform: function
        super:  <ggproto object: Class GuideNone, Guide, gg>
    package_box: function
    print: function
    process_layers: function
    setup: function
    subset_guides: function
    train: function
    update_params: function
    super:  <ggproto object: Class Guides, gg> 
 @ mapping    : <ggplot2::mapping>  Named list()
 @ theme      : <theme>  Named list()
 .. @ complete: logi FALSE
 .. @ validate: logi TRUE
 @ coordinates:Classes 'CoordCartesian', 'Coord', 'ggproto', 'gg' <ggproto object: Class CoordCartesian, Coord, gg>
    aspect: function
    backtransform_range: function
    clip: on
    default: TRUE
    distance: function
    draw_panel: function
    expand: TRUE
    is_free: function
    is_linear: function
    labels: function
    limits: list
    modify_scales: function
    range: function
    ratio: NULL
    render_axis_h: function
    render_axis_v: function
    render_bg: function
    render_fg: function
    reverse: none
    setup_data: function
    setup_layout: function
    setup_panel_guides: function
    setup_panel_params: function
    setup_params: function
    train_panel_guides: function
    transform: function
    super:  <ggproto object: Class CoordCartesian, Coord, gg> 
 @ facet      :Classes 'FacetNull', 'Facet', 'ggproto', 'gg' <ggproto object: Class FacetNull, Facet, gg>
    attach_axes: function
    attach_strips: function
    compute_layout: function
    draw_back: function
    draw_front: function
    draw_labels: function
    draw_panel_content: function
    draw_panels: function
    finish_data: function
    format_strip_labels: function
    init_gtable: function
    init_scales: function
    map_data: function
    params: list
    set_panel_size: function
    setup_data: function
    setup_panel_params: function
    setup_params: function
    shrink: TRUE
    train_scales: function
    vars: function
    super:  <ggproto object: Class FacetNull, Facet, gg> 
 @ layout     :Classes 'Layout', 'ggproto', 'gg' <ggproto object: Class Layout, gg>
    coord: NULL
    coord_params: list
    facet: NULL
    facet_params: list
    finish_data: function
    get_scales: function
    layout: NULL
    map_position: function
    panel_params: NULL
    panel_scales_x: NULL
    panel_scales_y: NULL
    render: function
    render_labels: function
    reset_scales: function
    resolve_label: function
    setup: function
    setup_panel_guides: function
    setup_panel_params: function
    train_position: function
    super:  <ggproto object: Class Layout, gg> 
 @ labels     : <ggplot2::labels>  Named list()
 @ meta       : list()
 @ plot_env   :<environment: R_GlobalEnv> 

 In this first chunk, we see that the data slot of the ggplot object now contains the underlying data.frame object. If we scroll past, we can spot additional slots, which are currently empty, waiting to be filled. We will sporadically check back and see how this changes.
So, we have stablish the first layer of our plot (Data). Now, we have to specify the next layer: aesthetics. In our case, we were interested in placing timepoint on the x-axis, and placing the TcellFrequency on the y-axis.

```{r}
#| eval: false
Plot <- ggplot(Data) + aes(x=timepoint, y=TcellFrequency)
Plot
```
 (now we get a grey square, like before, and the x and y axis with white lines).

If we glanced behind the curtains, we now see the mapping slot has now been filled in:

```{r}
#| eval: false
str(Plot)
```
(I paste just the interesting fragment of the data obtained)
@ mapping    : <ggplot2::mapping> List of 2
 .. $ x: language ~timepoint
 ..  ..- attr(*, ".Environment")=<environment: R_GlobalEnv> 
 .. $ y: language ~TcellFrequency
 ..  ..- attr(*, ".Environment")=<environment: R_GlobalEnv> 

 IMPORTANT NOTE: We should not put "" (quotation marks) around timepoint and TcellFrequency, because we use tidyverse to determine that they are columns and not objects. If we put quotation marks around them, we will obtain the two axis using those names as values (object) in the graphic.

 Now we have to add a new layer: geometrics. We have to decide what geometries we want to use. This is the layer that determines how our data is displayed/plotted.
 
 ```{r}
 #| eval: false
 ggplot(Data) + aes(x=timepoint, y=TcellFrequency) + geom_point()
 ```
 ![](C:/Users/Macarena/MacarenaLM/notes/week06 Visualizing with ggplot2/ggplot2 scatter plot 1.png){width=50%}

 We could swap geom_point() out for geom_boxplot():
 
 ```{r}
 #| eval: false
 ggplot(Data) + aes(x=timepoint, y=TcellFrequency) + geom_boxplot()
 ```
 ![](C:/Users/Macarena/MacarenaLM/notes/week06 Visualizing with ggplot2/ggplot2 box plot 1.png){width=50%}

```{r}
#| eval: false
ggplot(Data) + aes(x=timepoint, y=TcellFrequency) + geom_violin()
```
 ![](C:/Users/Macarena/MacarenaLM/notes/week06 Visualizing with ggplot2/ggplot2 violin plot 1.png){width=50%}

We could also add two geometries

```{r}
#| eval: false
ggplot(Data) + aes(x=timepoint, y=TcellFrequency) + geom_boxplot() + geom_point()
```
![](C:/Users/Macarena/MacarenaLM/notes/week06 Visualizing with ggplot2/ggplot2 box plot 2.png){width=50%}

As you can see, many different geom_ functions exist in both ggplot2, as well as other R packages that expand the plotting capacity further. One I like to use for my own plots is the ggbeeswarm package, specifically the geom_beeswarm() function in conjunction with the geom_boxplots().

```{r}
#| eval: false
install.packages("ggbeeswarm")
```

```{r}
#| eval: false
library(ggbeeswarm)
Plot <- ggplot(Data) + aes(x=timepoint, y=TcellFrequency) + geom_boxplot() + geom_beeswarm()
Plot
```
![](C:/Users/Macarena/MacarenaLM/notes/week06 Visualizing with ggplot2/ggplot2 box plot 3.png){width=50%}

In the case of the above dataset, we had converted timepoint to a factor. But what would our plot have looked like if we had left it in the original continuous format?

```{r}
#| eval: false
AlternateData <- read.csv(TheCSV, check.names=FALSE)
AlternateData <- AlternateData |> 
    mutate(TcellProportion=Tcells_count/CD45_count) |>
    mutate(TcellFrequency=TcellProportion *100) |>
    mutate(TcellFrequency=round(TcellFrequency, 1))
ggplot(AlternateData) + aes(x=timepoint, y=TcellFrequency) + geom_beeswarm()
```
![](C:/Users/Macarena/MacarenaLM/notes/week06 Visualizing with ggplot2/ggplot2 scatter plot 2.png){width=50%}

 In this case, the x-axis instead of being spaced categorically, now appears as continuous, with the individual dots being plotted at 0, 4, 9 respectively. Given that geom_boxplot is expecting a categorical factor, this fails to plot correctly when added on:
 
 ```{r}
 #| eval: false
 ggplot(AlternateData) + aes(x=timepoint, y=TcellFrequency) + geom_boxplot() + geom_beeswarm()
 ```
 Warning messages:
1: Orientation is not uniquely specified when both the x and
y aesthetics are continuous. Picking default orientation
'x'. 
2: Continuous x aesthetic
ℹ did you forget `aes(group = ...)`? 

![](C:/Users/Macarena/MacarenaLM/notes/week06 Visualizing with ggplot2/ggplot2 box plot 4.png){width=50%}

Fortunately, the warning message provides some context we can use when troubleshooting. If we implement the suggestion, we are able to rescue geom_boxplot() plotting attempt even in abscence of specifying timepoint as categorical.

```{r}
#| eval: false
ggplot(AlternateData) + aes(x=timepoint, y=TcellFrequency, group=timepoint) + geom_boxplot() + geom_beeswarm()
```
![](C:/Users/Macarena/MacarenaLM/notes/week06 Visualizing with ggplot2/ggplot2 box plot 5.png){width=50%}

This example generally highlights the importance of being aware of what types of values you are trying to plot, warning messages, and some of the quiky odd-looking plots that can arise when the assumptions are not met.

# Mismatched Assumptions
In this case, the two “geom_” functions we are using are using x-axis value as categorical, and y-axis as a continuous variable. What happens if we used a “geom_” function that has different specifications?

```{r}
#| eval: false
ggplot(Data) + aes(x=timepoint, y=TcellFrequency) + geom_histogram() 
```

Error in `geom_histogram()`:
! Problem while computing stat.
ℹ Error occurred in the 1st layer.
Caused by error in `setup_params()`:
! `stat_bin()` must only have an x or y aesthetic.

In the case of the above, geom_histogram() was expecting only one column being present in the aes() argument.

```{r}
#| eval: false
ggplot(Data) + aes(x=TcellFrequency) + geom_histogram() 
```
`stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value
`binwidth`.
![](C:/Users/Macarena/MacarenaLM/notes/week06 Visualizing with ggplot2/ggplot2 bar chart 1.png){width=50%}

This would similarly be the case for geom_density()

```{r}
#| eval: false
ggplot(Data) + aes(x=TcellFrequency) + geom_density() 
```
![](C:/Users/Macarena/MacarenaLM/notes/week06 Visualizing with ggplot2/ggplot2 density plot 1.png){width=50%}

Let's back to our current beeswarm boxplot:

```{r}
#| eval: false
Plot <- ggplot(Data) + aes(x=timepoint, y=TcellFrequency) + geom_boxplot() + geom_beeswarm()
Plot
```
![](C:/Users/Macarena/MacarenaLM/notes/week06 Visualizing with ggplot2/ggplot2 box plot 3.png){width=50%}

If we are making any large changes to the geometry layer, it would be occuring as an argument provided to the geometry determining functions. Lets check to see what our argument options are for both of the above.

```{r}
?geom_boxplot
```

```{r}
?geom_beeswarm
```
Currently, all the dots in the beeswarm are kind of small, and tightly wrapped together around the vertical axis for their respective groups. Let’s attempt to change this. One of the easiest things to do when modifying arguments is to start with size (or the equivalent argument)

```{r}
ggplot(Data) + aes(x=timepoint, y=TcellFrequency) + 
    geom_boxplot() + geom_beeswarm(size=5)
```
![](C:/Users/Macarena/MacarenaLM/notes/week06 Visualizing with ggplot2/ggplot2 box plot 7.png){width=50%}

Let’s tone it back.

```{r}
ggplot(Data) + aes(x=timepoint, y=TcellFrequency) +
     geom_boxplot() + geom_beeswarm(size=2.5)
```
![](C:/Users/Macarena/MacarenaLM/notes/week06 Visualizing with ggplot2/ggplot2 box plot 5.png){width=50%}

Better, but now the individual dots being too tightly wrapped together is limiting our capacity to visualize individuals. Lets next attempt to get the dots to spread out more horizontally from each other. Taking a look at the documentation, it looks like “cex” is the argument that might work.

```{r}
ggplot(Data) + aes(x=timepoint, y=TcellFrequency) + 
    geom_boxplot() + geom_beeswarm(size=2.5, cex=5)
```
![](C:/Users/Macarena/MacarenaLM/notes/week06 Visualizing with ggplot2/ggplot2 box plot 9.png){width=50%}

Too far! Let’s adjust it again

```{r}
ggplot(Data) + aes(x=timepoint, y=TcellFrequency) + 
    geom_boxplot() + geom_beeswarm(size=2.5, cex=2.5)
```
![](C:/Users/Macarena/MacarenaLM/notes/week06 Visualizing with ggplot2/ggplot2 box plot 10.png){width=50%}

Good enough for now!
# Scales
The next ggplot2 layer is escales. We have seen that we are able to change the size of dots and the space among them. However, we can also modify their appearance at the scale level.
In our metadata, we can see that there is a column named "infant_sex", that stablish the sex origin of cells:

```{r}
Data |> pull(infant_sex) |> unique()
```
[1] "Male"   "Female"

Let’s go ahead and attempt to alter the dots to reflect based on this additional metadata variable. First, we can assign values based on the categorical values that are present for that column.

```{r}
shape_sex <- c("Female" = 22, "Male" = 21)
fill_sex <- c("Female" = "white", "Male" = "black")
```
The two scale layer functions we will use will be scale_shape_manual() and scale_fill_manual().

```{r}
ggplot(Data) + aes(x=timepoint, y=TcellFrequency) + 
    geom_boxplot() + geom_beeswarm(size=2.5, cex=2.5) +
    scale_shape_manual(shape=shape_sex) + scale_fill_manual(fill=fill_sex)
```

Error in `discrete_scale()`:
! unused argument (shape = c(22, 21))

This did not quite work as expected, with our additional arguments not being perceived by the plot. Part of this is due to needing to set up the underlying geometry layer with the necessary arguments as well. After some investigation, lets try

```{r}
ggplot(Data) + aes(x=timepoint, y=TcellFrequency) + 
    geom_boxplot() + geom_beeswarm(size=2.5, cex=2.5, aes(shape=infant_sex, fill=infant_sex)) +
    scale_shape_manual(values=shape_sex) + scale_fill_manual(values=fill_sex)
```
![](C:/Users/Macarena/MacarenaLM/notes/week06 Visualizing with ggplot2/ggplot2 box plot 11.png){width=50%}

# Facets
At this point, we have added the Data, Aesthetic, and two Geometry layers. The next layer, Facets, allows creation of separate plots based on an additional variable. For example, if we were interested in seeing the difference between male and female infrants, we could specify the column name within the facet_wrap() argument.

```{r}
ggplot(Data) + aes(x=timepoint, y=TcellFrequency) +
     geom_boxplot() + geom_beeswarm(size=2.5, cex=2.5, aes(shape=infant_sex, fill=infant_sex)) +
     scale_shape_manual(values=shape_sex) + scale_fill_manual(values=fill_sex) + facet_wrap(~ infant_sex)
```
![](C:/Users/Macarena/MacarenaLM/notes/week06 Visualizing with ggplot2/ggplot2 box plot 12.png){width=50%}

We could similarly return individual plots for various treatment conditions (Ctrl, PPD, SEB)

```{r}
ggplot(Data) + aes(x=timepoint, y=TcellFrequency) +
     geom_boxplot() + geom_beeswarm(size=2.5, cex=2.5, aes(shape=infant_sex, fill=infant_sex)) +
     scale_shape_manual(values=shape_sex) + scale_fill_manual(values=fill_sex) + facet_wrap(~ Condition)
```
![](C:/Users/Macarena/MacarenaLM/notes/week06 Visualizing with ggplot2/ggplot2 box plot 13.png){width=50%}
